next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

Joel Beckman had been counting the hours since anyone last asked him about the argument. Fourteen. A personal best for this weekend, though the bar was roughly ankle-high. The previous record had been six hours, overnight, because even these people had to sleep eventually.

Joel sat at the kitchen table with a turkey sandwich he wasn't eating and watched Nate try to scrape the last of the mustard from the jar with a butter knife. Cliff was at the counter cutting an apple into mathematically precise slices. Tommy was on his second sandwich, maybe his third. Joel had stopped tracking. Paul sat across from him with a cup of coffee he kept warming in the microwave, the same cup he'd been nursing since morning, which Joel found either disciplined or insane.

Doug was eating a bowl of cereal, because Doug had apparently decided cereal was a valid Sunday lunch option and nobody had the energy to argue.

The chair at the head of the table was empty. Nobody had mentioned it. Everyone had noticed.

"Mustard's done," Nate announced, holding up the jar. "We're officially post-mustard. I want that noted for the record."

"There's more in the garage fridge," Paul said.

"The garage fridge. Of course. Because Martin had a condiment annex." Nate set the jar down and took a bite of his dry sandwich. After a while he said, "His garage fridge had better beer than my actual fridge. That's not a bit. That's just true."

The kitchen was quiet except for the refrigerator's cycling hum and Tommy's methodical chewing. Afternoon light came through the windows at a low angle and caught d...
open_hooks_to_carry: Cliff's financial motive: Martin owed him forty thousand from a failed joint real estate deal
The safe: wall-mounted, combination lock, Martin left the dial one tick off
The manila envelope labeled RT — Cliff expects real estate documents
Martin's handwritten account of the 2002 cabin fire in Washburn County, Wisconsin
The account names Tommy as the one who started the fire; names Ray Toomey as the caretaker who died
Cliff reads it twice, puts it back, and the reader should feel the temperature change
prev_tail_excerpt:
Mallick finally tucked the notebook shut against her palm.

"Well. Thank you, gentlemen. I appreciate it. And I'm sorry again for the marching around. Pretty soon you'll all be rid of me."

Nobody said that seemed likely.

She went back upstairs. Her steps faded. The house settled around them again.

Nate lowered his phone.

"You leaving?" he asked.

"No."

"Okay."

That was one of the reasons Doug could stand Nate in doses. Nate could talk for an hour when he was scared, but if he heard the floor drop out under a person he usually stopped.

Doug set the duffel on his sofa. For a second he thought about moving the bottles again, somewhere smaller, somewhere dumber, the laundry hamper or behind the extra paper towels or under the sink in the bathroom. Every option felt more suspicious than the last. He unzipped the side pocket just far enough to make sure both bottles were still there and zipped it shut again.

Nate looked away on purpose and gave him that much privacy.

"You want a smoke later?" Nate said.

"Maybe."

"All right."

Doug kicked off his shoes and lay back on the pull-out fully dressed, the duffel against his hip because now that Mallick had seen him moving it, putting it anywhere else felt like another decision he would have to make for a reason. Above his head the refrigerator kicked on, rattled through its cycle, clicked off. Before it started again, he still hadn't slept.
secondary_character_beats: Cliff's financial panic should be legible as genuine desperation, not greed — his business is failing and this money matters

setups_to_plant.description: The handwritten account of the 2002 fire — Martin's record of Tommy starting the fire that killed Ray Toomey. Cliff finds it, photographs it, and replaces it. This document drives the murder motive and the resolution.

state_deltas_required: Cliff's financial desperation driving him to Martin's safe, where he finds something far more valuable than money
Cliff holds the most dangerous secret in the house — leverage that he does not yet understand is lethal
